---
title: "Physics-Informed Neural Networks"
subtitle: "Differentiable Physical Modelling and Machine Learning for Sound Synthesis"
author: "Rodrigo Diaz"
date: "2026-06-11"
execute-dir: file
execute:
  cache: false
  warning: false
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: dark
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: true
    smaller: true
    scrollable: true
    code-fold: false
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
    footer: "Week 6 -- Physics-Informed Neural Networks"
    transition: fade
    math: mathjax
    auto-play-media: false
---

## Overview

::: {.incremental}
- Recap: where Neural ODEs put the physics
- PINNs as physics-informed **losses**, not special architectures
- Coordinate networks for fields $w(\mathbf{x},t)$
- Data, boundary, initial, and residual losses
- Worked notation: damped wave equation and damped oscillator
- Inverse problems: learning physical parameters from sparse observations
- Practical limits for sound synthesis
:::

::: {.notes}
Week 5 focused on making the time evolution learnable. This week shifts the question: instead of learning a vector field and then integrating it, we learn a whole space-time function and penalise violations of the differential equation.
:::

# From Neural ODEs to PINNs

## Week 5 Recap

Neural ODEs describe time evolution through a state-space model:

$$
\dot{\mathbf h}(t)
=
\mathbf F_\theta(\mathbf h(t),t,f(t))
$$

. . .

The solver produces the trajectory:

$$
\mathbf h(0)
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
\mathbf h(t_1),\mathbf h(t_2),\dots
$$

. . .

The physics lives inside the vector field or the numerical integrator.

## The PINN Shift

PINNs do not first build a time-stepping simulator.

. . .

They fit a function directly:

$$
(\mathbf x,t)
\quad\longmapsto\quad
w_\theta(\mathbf x,t)
$$

. . .

Then they ask:

$$
\text{Does this function satisfy the equation?}
$$

## Same Ingredients, Different Place

| | Neural ODE | PINN |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Learns | vector field or residual force | space-time solution field |
| Input | state $\mathbf h(t)$, time, forcing | coordinates $(\mathbf x,t)$ |
| Physics appears in | dynamics / solver | loss function |
| Main derivative | $d\mathbf h/dt$ during integration | derivatives of $w_\theta$ with respect to inputs |
| Natural output | trajectory | continuous field |

. . .

Both depend on automatic differentiation, but they differentiate different things.

# Physics-Informed Losses

## The Core Idea

Suppose the physical system is written as:

$$
\mathcal N[w](\mathbf x,t) = f(\mathbf x,t),
\qquad
(\mathbf x,t)\in \Omega\times[0,T]
$$

. . .

For a neural approximation $w_\theta$, define the residual:

$$
\mathcal R_\theta(\mathbf x,t)
=
\mathcal N[w_\theta](\mathbf x,t)
-
f(\mathbf x,t)
$$

. . .

If $\mathcal R_\theta=0$ everywhere, the network output satisfies the PDE.

## What Is Being Differentiated?

The network input contains coordinates:

$$
w_\theta(\mathbf x,t)
=
\operatorname{MLP}_\theta(\mathbf x,t)
$$

. . .

Automatic differentiation gives:

$$
\partial_t w_\theta,\quad
\partial_t^2 w_\theta,\quad
\nabla w_\theta,\quad
\Delta w_\theta,\quad
\dots
$$

. . .

These are derivatives of the **output with respect to the input coordinates**, not just derivatives with respect to the network parameters.

## Why This Is Useful

We can train from constraints, not only from dense labelled trajectories.

. . .

The model can see:

::: {.incremental}
- a few measured samples
- boundary conditions
- initial conditions
- collocation points with no measured target
- known forcing
- unknown physical parameters treated as trainable variables
:::

. . .

The PDE fills in information between sparse observations.

# The PINN Objective

## Four Loss Terms

For a field $w(\mathbf x,t)$, a useful decomposition is:

$$
\mathcal L(\theta)
=
\lambda_{\mathrm{data}}\mathcal L_{\mathrm{data}}
+
\lambda_{\mathrm{ic}}\mathcal L_{\mathrm{ic}}
+
\lambda_{\mathrm{bc}}\mathcal L_{\mathrm{bc}}
+
\lambda_{\mathrm{phys}}\mathcal L_{\mathrm{phys}}
$$

. . .

The $\lambda$ weights matter. They decide which constraints the optimiser listens to.

## Data Loss

At measured points $(\mathbf x_m,t_m)$:

$$
\mathcal L_{\mathrm{data}}
=
\frac{1}{M}
\sum_{m=1}^{M}
\left|
w_\theta(\mathbf x_m,t_m)-w_m
\right|^2
$$

. . .

For audio, the measurement may be a readout rather than the full displacement field:

$$
y_\theta(t_m)
=
\mathbf C\!\left[w_\theta(\cdot,t_m)\right]
$$

. . .

Then compare $y_\theta(t_m)$ to the recorded signal $y_m$.

## Initial Condition Loss

For a vibrating object:

$$
w(\mathbf x,0)=w_0(\mathbf x),
\qquad
\partial_t w(\mathbf x,0)=v_0(\mathbf x)
$$

. . .

The PINN loss becomes:

$$
\mathcal L_{\mathrm{ic}}
=
\left\|w_\theta(\mathbf x,0)-w_0(\mathbf x)\right\|^2
+
\left\|\partial_t w_\theta(\mathbf x,0)-v_0(\mathbf x)\right\|^2
$$

## Boundary Condition Loss

Fixed string ends:

$$
w(0,t)=0,
\qquad
w(L,t)=0
$$

. . .

Penalty form:

$$
\mathcal L_{\mathrm{bc}}
=
\left\|w_\theta(0,t)\right\|^2
+
\left\|w_\theta(L,t)\right\|^2
$$

. . .

Some constraints can also be built into the network output, but today we keep the simple penalty view.

## Physics Residual Loss

Choose collocation points:

$$
(\mathbf x_r,t_r)\sim \Omega\times[0,T]
$$

. . .

Evaluate the residual at those points:

$$
\mathcal L_{\mathrm{phys}}
=
\frac{1}{R}
\sum_{r=1}^{R}
\left|
\mathcal R_\theta(\mathbf x_r,t_r)
\right|^2
$$

. . .

No measured displacement is needed at these points.

## Training Recipe

::: {.incremental}
1. Sample measured data points.
2. Sample initial and boundary points.
3. Sample residual collocation points in the domain.
4. Evaluate $w_\theta$.
5. Use automatic differentiation to compute the required derivatives.
6. Form the residual $\mathcal R_\theta$.
7. Minimise the weighted loss.
:::

. . .

The simulator is replaced by a differentiable residual calculation.

# Example: Damped Wave Equation

## Equation From Earlier Weeks

For a damped driven string:

$$
\partial_t^2 w
=
\hat T^2\,\partial_x^2 w
-
2\hat d_1\,\partial_t w
+
b(x)f(t)
$$

. . .

Here:

::: {.incremental}
- $w(x,t)$ is transverse displacement
- $\hat T$ is wave speed
- $\hat d_1$ is damping
- $s(x,t)=b(x)f(t)$ is the source term
- for now, assume $s(x,t)$ is known
:::

## PINN Approximation

Replace the unknown displacement with:

$$
w(x,t)
\approx
w_\theta(x,t)
$$

. . .

For this equation, the differential operator is:

$$
\mathcal N[w]
=
\left[
\partial_t^2
-
\hat T^2\,\partial_x^2
+
2\hat d_1\,\partial_t
\right]w
$$

. . .

so the PDE is:

$$
\mathcal N[w](x,t)
=
s(x,t),
\qquad
s(x,t)=b(x)f(t)
$$

. . .

The residual is:

$$
\mathcal R_\theta(x,t)
=
\mathcal N[w_\theta](x,t)
-
s(x,t)
$$

. . .

Training pushes $\mathcal R_\theta(x,t)$ toward zero across the space-time domain.

## What Happens To The Force?

In the forward PINN setup, the source is given:

$$
s(x,t)=b(x)f(t)
$$

. . .

Examples:

::: {.incremental}
- free vibration: $s(x,t)=0$
- measured actuator force: $f(t)$ is known
- known excitation position: $b(x)$ is fixed
:::

. . .

Then the network only estimates the field $w_\theta(x,t)$.

## If The Force Is Unknown

Then it becomes an inverse-source problem.

. . .

Introduce a source model:

$$
s_\psi(x,t)=b_\alpha(x)f_\psi(t)
$$

. . .

and optimise:

$$
\mathcal R_{\theta,\psi}(x,t)
=
\mathcal N[w_\theta](x,t)
-
s_\psi(x,t)
$$

. . .

This needs extra assumptions. From one audio signal, source shape, source position, and material parameters can trade off against each other.

## Boundary and Initial Constraints

For a fixed string:

$$
w_\theta(0,t)=0,
\qquad
w_\theta(L,t)=0
$$

. . .

For a plucked initial condition:

$$
w_\theta(x,0)=w_0(x),
\qquad
\partial_t w_\theta(x,0)=0
$$

. . .

These are not extra simulation steps. They are additional loss terms.

## What Does The Network Learn?

It learns a continuous surface:

$$
(x,t)\mapsto w_\theta(x,t)
$$

. . .

After training, we can query:

::: {.incremental}
- any time $t$ in the trained interval
- any position $x$ in the trained spatial domain
- output positions not explicitly sampled during training
- derivatives such as velocity $\partial_t w_\theta(x,t)$
:::

. . .

This is attractive for sparse reconstruction, but expensive compared with modal or finite-difference rollout.

# Warm-Up: Damped Oscillator

## Zero-Dimensional Analogue

For one modal coordinate:

$$
\ddot q(t)
+
2\gamma \dot q(t)
+
\omega_0^2 q(t)
=
f(t)
$$

. . .

PINN approximation:

$$
q(t)\approx q_\theta(t)
$$

. . .

Residual:

$$
\mathcal R_\theta(t)
=
\ddot q_\theta(t)
+
2\gamma \dot q_\theta(t)
+
\omega_0^2 q_\theta(t)
-
f(t)
$$

## Enforcing the ODE

![](images/PINNAnimation.mp4){fig-align="center"}

## Training Process

![](images/pinn_training.webm){fig-align="center"}

::: {.notes}
Use this as the simplest visual example: the network is not rolled out like an ODE solver. It is shaped by data, initial conditions, and residual penalties until its graph satisfies the ODE.
:::

## Companion Notebook

The runnable Week 6 example is:

```sh
uv run python week06/notebooks/pinn_harmonic_oscillator.py
```

. . .

It fits $q_\theta(t)$ from sparse early samples, initial conditions, and the oscillator residual while also learning $\sigma_2$ and $\omega_0$.

# Inverse Problems

## Learning Unknown Physical Parameters

Separate network parameters from physical parameters:

$$
\theta
=
\text{network weights},
\qquad
\boldsymbol\eta
=
\text{physical coefficients}
$$

. . .

For example:

$$
\boldsymbol\eta
=
\{\hat T,\hat d_1,\hat D,K\}
$$

. . .

Now define:

$$
\mathcal R_{\theta,\boldsymbol\eta}
=
\mathcal N_{\boldsymbol\eta}[w_\theta]-f
$$

and optimise both $\theta$ and $\boldsymbol\eta$.

## Audio Motivation

Recordings are usually sparse observations of the field:

$$
y(t)
=
\mathbf C[w(\cdot,t)]
$$

. . .

The inverse question is:

$$
\text{Which physical parameters explain this measured sound?}
$$

. . .

PINNs are useful when the data alone is too sparse, but the governing equation is trusted.

## What Must Be Identifiable?

The optimiser can only recover parameters that leave a detectable signature in the observations.

. . .

For audio this depends on:

::: {.incremental}
- excitation position and spectrum
- readout position
- signal duration
- damping and noise
- whether multiple parameter settings produce similar spectra
- whether the boundary and forcing assumptions are correct
:::

. . .

A physics-informed loss does not remove observability problems.

# Practical Caveats

## Why PINNs Are Hard To Train

::: {.incremental}
- The residual can contain high-order derivatives.
- Oscillatory solutions are difficult for standard MLPs.
- Loss terms can have incompatible scales.
- Collocation sampling changes what the model prioritises.
- Optimisers may satisfy boundary conditions while missing wave propagation.
- Long time intervals amplify small residual errors.
:::

## Spectral Bias

Standard MLPs tend to learn smooth, low-frequency structure first.

. . .

That is a problem for sound:

::: {.incremental}
- high modes matter perceptually
- attacks contain broadband transients
- stiff strings and plates are dispersive
- damping can hide modelling errors in short clips
:::

. . .

Architectures with Fourier features or sinusoidal activations often help, but they do not make training automatic.

## PINNs Are Usually Not Real-Time Synthesisers

Evaluating a trained PINN at audio rate can be expensive.

. . .

Training is much more expensive:

::: {.incremental}
- many collocation points
- repeated automatic differentiation
- high precision often helps
- inverse problems require repeated residual evaluations
:::

. . .

For real-time audio, PINNs are usually better as analysis, fitting, or model-discovery tools.

## When They Make Sense

Use PINNs when:

::: {.incremental}
- measurements are sparse
- the governing equation is trusted
- boundary or initial conditions matter
- unknown physical parameters are the target
- you want a continuous field, not just an audio signal
:::

. . .

Use modal, FDTD, state-space, or neural ODE methods when the goal is fast rollout.

# Summary

## Week 6 Takeaway

PINNs fit a coordinate function:

$$
w_\theta(\mathbf x,t)
$$

. . .

and train it with:

$$
\mathcal L
=
\lambda_{\mathrm{data}}\mathcal L_{\mathrm{data}}
+
\lambda_{\mathrm{ic}}\mathcal L_{\mathrm{ic}}
+
\lambda_{\mathrm{bc}}\mathcal L_{\mathrm{bc}}
+
\lambda_{\mathrm{phys}}\left\|\mathcal R_\theta\right\|^2
$$

. . .

The physics is not in a solver loop. It is in the residual.

## The Main Tradeoff

::: {.incremental}
- Strong prior: the equation constrains the learned field.
- Sparse data: measurements do not need to cover the full domain.
- Inverse problems: physical coefficients can be optimised directly.
- Cost: training is slow and sensitive.
- Audio caveat: high-frequency, long-time behaviour is especially demanding.
:::

## Bridge Forward

PINNs learn one solution field for one problem setup.

. . .

Next question:

$$
\text{Can we learn the operator that maps problem setup to solution?}
$$

. . .

That leads toward neural operators and other models that learn families of PDE solutions.

## Other PINN Variants

The same idea can be combined with different physical structure.

::: {.incremental}
- **Lagrangian PINNs:** encode equations through an action or variational principle.
- **Hamiltonian PINNs:** preserve energy-based structure for conservative systems.
- **Port-Hamiltonian PINNs:** include energy flow, dissipation, and external ports.
- **Domain-decomposed PINNs:** split hard space-time domains into smaller pieces.
:::

. . .

The common theme is the same: choose a structure, then make violations of that structure trainable.

## Resources

- Raissi, Perdikaris, Karniadakis (2019), original PINN paper: <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcp.2018.10.045>
- Moseley, Markham, Nissen-Meyer (2023), Finite Basis PINNs: <https://doi.org/10.1007/s10444-023-10065-9>
- Lagrangian PINNs: Mojgani, Balajewicz, Hassanzadeh (2022): <https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.02902>
- Hamiltonian neural networks: Greydanus, Dzamba, Yosinski (2019): <https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper/2019/hash/26cd8ecadce0d4efd6cc8a8725cbd1f8-Abstract.html>
- Port-Hamiltonian neural networks: Desai et al. (2021): <https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevE.104.034312>
